draft.introduction: Saadia Gaon’s Book of Beliefs and Opinions (933 CE) is the first systematic Jewish philosophy, written in Arabic as Kitāb al-Amānāt wa-l-I‘tiqādāt. It defends the rationality of revelation and establishes that reason and scripture, properly understood, can never contradict each other.

translation.original_ref: Saadia Gaon, Book of Beliefs and Opinions
translation.text: Blessed be God, the God of Israel, who is truly and certainly existent, who confirms by proof the existence of our souls and the existence of every truth. He has established for us a gateway to the knowledge of truth through the senses, through reason, through inference, and through authentic tradition. And we say that these four sources of knowledge are roots from which all human knowledge grows.

translator_notes.note: Saadia’s four sources of knowledge—sense perception, rational intuition, inference, and reliable tradition—established the epistemological framework for all subsequent Jewish philosophy. The inclusion of “authentic tradition” as a source of knowledge alongside reason was innovative: it legitimized revelation within a rational framework rather than opposing them.
